explicit_text: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first heard from Sergeant Dan Beck that a plane had hit one of the towers of the World Trade Center in New York. At first, I thought it might have been an accident, but when a second plane hit the other tower, it became clear this was more than just a freak incident. Watching the events unfold on TV while working, I realized the gravity of the situation as the towers were attacked.

Watching the events unfold on TV while working, I realized the gravity of the situation as those iconic skyscrapers were attacked.

explicit_text: I was in New York City on September 11, 2001, when the World Trade Center was attacked. The chaos and confusion were palpable as news came in about planes hitting the towers. Although I was not physically there, the impact of the attacks on New York City was central to the unfolding national crisis that day.

explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I arrived at the Pentagon within minutes after it was hit by a plane. The destruction was unlike anything I had ever seen, with heavy fire and debris scattered near the heliport side where the damage occurred. We helped the fire department with hose lines and assisted injured people, while military personnel and firefighters worked tirelessly to rescue survivors despite the dangerous conditions.

explicit_text: I experienced the September 11 attacks firsthand, starting with the news of the planes hitting the World Trade Center and then rushing to the Pentagon after it was also attacked. The day was filled with confusion, shock, and urgent response efforts as multiple sites were hit. We coordinated escorts for officials and emergency personnel, and I witnessed the bravery of military and fire department members amid the chaos.

explicit_text: While heading to the Pentagon on September 11, 2001, I noticed a military plane circling the smoke above the building. It looked like a C-130 Hercules, though I’m not an expert on aircraft. The plane circled a few times before flying off south, which added to the tense atmosphere as we responded to the attack.

explicit_text: On the morning of September 11, 2001, the President was scheduled to fly from the Pentagon heliport back to the White House around 11:30. Our unit was tasked with escorting him, but after the attacks, the situation changed drastically. The White House became part of the broader security concerns as the day unfolded.
